subject: Careers
topic: The Social Dilemma: Algorithms, Attention, and Cognitive Bias

secondary_architecture_support.note: If needed, the lesson can split at the 47-minute midpoint into two shorter class sessions without changing the companion packet or post-film options.

materials_control_note: The student companion holds only pre-viewing, follow-along, and post-viewing responses. The answer key, timing notes, and activity menu stay teacher-facing.
grade_subject_fit: Fits Grade 8 Careers by linking digital environments, self-awareness, decision-making, media influence, and responsible online choices to students' current school and social lives.
unit_context: This lesson follows a short mini-unit on cognitive bias. Students use the documentary as a case study for how digital platforms shape attention, emotions, decisions, and beliefs.
assignment_purpose: Students watch The Social Dilemma, identify persuasive platform design choices, connect those choices to cognitive bias and vulnerable decision-making, and reflect on healthier digital habits and more humane technology design.
final_evidence_target: A short post-film exit response that names one design choice from the film, connects it to a likely cognitive bias or vulnerability, and proposes one healthier response or redesign.
student_task_flow: Review key cognitive bias terms before viewing so the film has an analytical lens, not just a reaction lens.
Use the follow-along packet to capture major ideas from the documentary at approximate timestamps.
Connect documentary examples to persuasive design, misinformation, mental health, polarization, and algorithmic bias.
Complete a short post-film planning response and discuss one practical action students, designers, schools, or families could take.
success_criteria: I can identify at least two persuasive design choices described in the film.
I can connect examples from the film to at least one cognitive bias or behavioral vulnerability.
I can explain one possible effect of algorithmic systems on attention, emotions, or beliefs.
I can propose one realistic healthier response, classroom norm, or design change.
supports_scaffolds: A before-viewing matching bank refreshes cognitive bias vocabulary from the previous mini-unit.
Timestamped follow-along questions keep students focused on major ideas instead of trying to write everything down.
Question types stay constrained to matching, multiple choice, and fill-in-the-blank so note-taking load stays manageable during a film.
A short post-film planner helps students move from reaction to reflection before discussion or follow-up work.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Pause briefly at a few key timestamps, review vocabulary aloud, and allow partner talk before students complete fill-in blanks.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Students complete the companion independently during the film, then use paired discussion and one follow-up activity after viewing.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Students compare which matters more in the documentary's argument: business model, algorithmic design, or user habits, and justify a position with evidence.

checkpoint_release_logic: Use quick partner checks after major sections of the film if students are losing the thread of the documentary argument.
Release one post-film Chromebook activity after students complete the companion and short debrief.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep the tone analytical rather than alarmist. Students should critique systems and habits without turning the conversation into simple tech panic.
Re-anchor often to the mini-unit on cognitive bias so students are naming patterns in thinking, not only listing movie facts.
Because this is a post-lunch double block, front-load the lens and expectations before pressing play. The packet should reduce drift during a long viewing period.
Treat the film as one perspective worth analyzing. Invite students to test claims, notice evidence, and consider limits as well as strengths.
likely_misconceptions: Students may assume the film says all technology is bad rather than critiquing particular business models and design choices.
Students may confuse cognitive bias with intentional lying; remind them that bias can shape perception without a person realizing it.
Students may treat algorithms as neutral math instead of systems trained on human choices, priorities, and data.
Students may think the only solution is individual willpower, missing the film's emphasis on design and accountability.
assessment_focus: Accuracy of key ideas captured during the film
Ability to connect examples to cognitive bias or behavioral vulnerability
Quality of post-film reasoning about healthier responses or humane redesign
Participation in reflective discussion or Chromebook follow-up

teacher_guide.big_idea: Digital platforms do not just host choices; they shape choices. Students can use cognitive bias language to notice how attention, emotion, and decision-making get steered online.
learning_goals: Use cognitive bias vocabulary to interpret examples from a documentary.
Identify how persuasive design can influence user attention and behavior.
Reflect on how online environments can affect personal well-being, beliefs, and choices.
Propose realistic changes in habits, norms, or design.
materials: Netflix access to The Social Dilemma

timing.activity: Launch the lesson, reconnect to cognitive bias, explain the companion packet, and preview key viewing questions.
Watch approximately the first 67 minutes of the film. Pause only if students need a reset or a quick clarity check.
Finish the final 27 minutes of the film.
Short partner or table-group debrief using the post-film planner.
Choose one Chromebook-ready follow-up activity from the teacher menu.
Whole-class closure and optional exit ticket.

teacher_notes: Timestamps are approximate and can shift slightly depending on Netflix playback version.
Preview the film for fit with your class and school context before use.
If attention drops, use brief processing pauses rather than frequent stop-and-start viewing.
Push students to separate documentary claim, documentary evidence, and their own judgment about whether the argument is convincing.
likely_hesitations: Some students may feel defensive if they think the lesson is simply telling them to stop using social media.
Some students may agree strongly with the film but struggle to explain why with evidence.
Some students may want to turn the lesson into personal confessions instead of analyzing systems and design.
teacher_moves: Keep asking: What is the platform trying to get the user to do next?
Ask: Which bias or vulnerability does this example seem to lean on?
When students make sweeping claims, follow with: What in the film made you say that?
Let students challenge the film too. The goal is analysis, not automatic agreement.
model_reflection_language: A platform can feel useful and still be designed to pull me back more often than I intended.
A design choice is not neutral just because it is common.
A bias does not make someone stupid; it makes them human and predictable.
look_fors: Student can describe at least one persuasive design feature clearly.
Student can connect a film example to confirmation bias, social proof, availability, loss aversion, or another vulnerability in thinking.
Student can name a likely consequence of the design choice beyond simply saying it is addictive.
Student can move from critique to one realistic response or redesign.
support_moves: Give students 20 to 30 seconds of partner talk before harder fill-in items.
Offer a short word bank for students who need vocabulary support: attention, behavior, engagement, bias, algorithm, profit, outrage, comparison.
Accept close synonyms on fill-in answers when the idea is sound.
future_pathway_connection: Connect the lesson to careers in technology, design, marketing, journalism, law, and public policy by asking who gets to decide how platforms are built and regulated.

post_film_activity_options.title: Bias Audit of a Hypothetical Feed
post_film_activity_options.student_prompt: In pairs, review a mock feed or teacher-provided screenshots and identify which posts seem designed to trigger comparison, outrage, fear of missing out, or confirmation bias.
post_film_activity_options.teacher_note: Students can record findings in a shared Google Doc or Slides deck.

post_film_activity_options.title: Humane Redesign Challenge
post_film_activity_options.student_prompt: Choose one feature from the documentary, such as notifications, autoplay, streaks, or recommendations, and redesign it to reduce manipulation while keeping it useful.
post_film_activity_options.teacher_note: Students can sketch in Google Drawings, Slides, or Canva if available.

post_film_activity_options.title: Claim-Test Response
post_film_activity_options.student_prompt: Choose one major claim from the documentary and write whether you think it is convincing, partly convincing, or overstated. Support your answer with examples from the film.
post_film_activity_options.teacher_note: Useful if you want a more skeptical media-literacy angle.

post_film_activity_options.title: Personal Tech Habit Reflection
post_film_activity_options.student_prompt: Write a short reflection about one online habit you want to keep, one you want to reduce, and one boundary or norm that could help.
post_film_activity_options.teacher_note: Keep private unless students choose to share.

post_film_activity_options.title: Careers in Humane Technology Mini-Inquiry
post_film_activity_options.student_prompt: Use your Chromebook to research one career that could shape healthier digital systems, such as UX designer, software engineer, journalist, digital policy analyst, or psychologist, and explain how that role could help.
post_film_activity_options.teacher_note: This keeps the lesson clearly tied to Careers outcomes.

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide
teacher_guide.overview: Use The Social Dilemma as a case study in how digital systems shape attention, emotion, and decision-making. The lesson works best when students use cognitive bias language as an analytical tool instead of treating the film as simple warning content.
teacher_guide.project_prompt: Students watch the documentary, track persuasive design examples, connect those examples to cognitive bias or behavioral vulnerability, and propose healthier responses or more humane redesigns.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Students should be able to name a design feature, connect it to a bias or vulnerability, and propose a realistic response or redesign.

model.sample: A platform can feel useful and still be designed to pull me back more often than I intended. One reason is that it uses rewards, notifications, and social comparison to keep attention engaged. That connects to confirmation bias or social proof because users are more likely to notice what already fits their feelings or what seems popular. A healthier redesign would slow the pressure to react instantly.

content.scenario: As you watch, keep asking: What is the platform trying to get the user to do next, and what human weakness or bias might that design be using?
prompts: What keeps you scrolling longer than you meant to?
When does technology feel like a tool, and when does it feel like it is steering you?

rows: Confirmation bias: noticing information that already fits what we think.
Social proof: following what looks popular, approved, or normal.
Availability heuristic and loss aversion: reacting strongly to what is vivid, shocking, or feels hard to miss.

items: Connect examples to bias and vulnerability

prompts: Which claim in the documentary felt strongest to you?
Which claim felt incomplete or overstated?
What realistic response or redesign seems most useful?

task_sheet.title: The Social Dilemma Video Companion
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use this companion before, during, and after the documentary.
instructions: Answer in the spaces provided while you watch.
Timestamps are approximate. Stay with the main idea even if playback is slightly ahead or behind.
Use matching, multiple choice, and fill-in-the-blank only.

tasks.label: Before Watching / Matching Bank
tasks.prompt: Match each cognitive bias term to the best description.

right_items: Feeling the pain of losing something more strongly than the pleasure of gaining something
Judging what is common or likely based on what comes to mind fastest
Following what seems popular, approved, or socially rewarded
Noticing and trusting information that already fits what you believe

tasks.label: Before Watching / Multiple Choice
tasks.prompt: Choose the best answer before the film starts.

render_hints.heading: What are you watching for?
choice_options: How persuasive design can shape attention and decisions
How to become a professional filmmaker
How to code a full social media app
How to memorize every fact in the documentary

render_hints.heading: Opening claim
choice_options: The companies mostly compete for user attention
The companies mostly sell school supplies
The companies mainly want users offline
The companies are neutral public services

render_hints.heading: Business model
blank_prompts: The film argues that platforms make money by predicting and influencing user ________.

tasks.label: While Watching / Matching Bank / 00:18-27:00
tasks.prompt: Match each platform goal to the best description.

right_items: Keep users returning and bringing in more users
Turn user attention and data into revenue
Keep users clicking, scrolling, and staying longer

render_hints.heading: Persuasive design features
choice_options: Infinite scroll and notifications encourage repeated checking
Printed maps encourage repeated checking
Locked paper notebooks encourage repeated checking
Library cards encourage repeated checking

render_hints.heading: Mental health effects
blank_prompts: The film connects heavy social comparison to rising ________ and lower well-being for some teens.

render_hints.heading: Misinformation and emotion
choice_options: False or extreme content often spreads because it gets stronger engagement
False content always spreads because it is longer
False content spreads only in textbooks
False content spreads because phones are heavier than books

tasks.label: While Watching / Matching Bank / 00:56-66:00
tasks.prompt: Match each example to the bias or vulnerability it most clearly connects to.

left_items: Seeing more posts that already fit your opinion
Clicking because everyone is talking about it
Remembering the most shocking story, not the most typical one
Refusing to stop scrolling because you might miss something
right_items: Loss aversion / fear of missing out

render_hints.heading: Algorithmic bias
blank_prompts: The film warns that algorithms are not neutral because they are trained on human ________ and unequal data.

render_hints.heading: Possible responses
choice_options: More humane design and stronger accountability
More persuasive tricks and more outrage
Less critical thinking in schools
More notifications for every user

render_hints.heading: Post-film planner
blank_prompts: One design choice that stood out to me was ________.
A bias or vulnerability it may use is ________.
One healthier response or redesign could be ________.

optional_extensions.label: If you finish early
optional_extensions.body: Add one sentence explaining whether the documentary's strongest argument is about business model, design, or user habits.

optional_extensions.label: If you finish early
optional_extensions.body: Choose one platform feature you use often and write whether it feels more like a tool or a trap, and why.

embedded_supports: If you miss an answer, leave it and catch the next one.
Use the timestamp as a guide, not as a reason to panic if playback is slightly off.
Focus on the film's argument, not on writing full sentences during every section.
success_criteria: I completed the companion using the key ideas from the film.
I connected at least one film example to a cognitive bias or vulnerability.
I used the post-film planner to move from reaction to reflection.
I stayed focused on the documentary's main argument instead of trying to copy everything.

quick_check_items: I identified at least one persuasive design choice.
I connected at least one example to bias or vulnerability.
I suggested one healthier response or redesign.

exit_ticket.title: Optional Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: Name one platform design choice from the film. Explain which bias or vulnerability it may use and one healthier response, norm, or redesign.
success_criteria: I named one design choice clearly.
I connected it to a bias or vulnerability in thinking or behavior.
I proposed one realistic response or redesign.
answer_key: Answers will vary. Strong responses name a specific design choice, connect it to a plausible bias or behavioral vulnerability, and propose a realistic action or redesign.
